idea.topic: M87* Event Horizon vs. Solar System
idea.hook: Is our ENTIRE Solar System actually tiny?
facts: The diameter of our Solar System, out to Neptune's orbit, is approximately 9 billion km (5.6 billion miles).
The Event Horizon (point of no return) of the supermassive black hole M87* is a staggering 38 billion km (23.6 billion miles) wide!
That means M87*'s event horizon is over 4 times WIDER than our entire Solar System!
idea.payoff: M87*'s event horizon could engulf our entire Solar System, including Neptune's orbit, more than 4 times over! We are truly a cosmic speck.
idea.title: Black Hole Shadow vs. Our Tiny Solar System! 😱

scenes.text: Is our ENTIRE Solar System tiny?
scenes.visual: A wide shot of our Solar System, slowly zooming out from Earth to show Jupiter, Saturn, etc. against a starry background.

scenes.text: Our Solar System (to Neptune): 9 BILLION km wide.
scenes.visual: An infographic showing the Solar System (Sun, planets, Neptune's orbit highlighted). A dotted line extends from one side to the other, indicating diameter.

scenes.text: M87* Event Horizon: 38 BILLION km wide!
scenes.visual: A dramatic artistic rendering of M87*'s event horizon, with a bright orange/yellow accretion disk surrounding a dark central void.

scenes.text: M87* is over 4x WIDER than our Solar System!
scenes.visual: A split screen. On the left, the Solar System graphic from Scene 2. On the right, a segment of the M87* event horizon. An 'X4' multiplier graphic appears between them.

scenes.text: M87* could engulf us 4+ times! We are cosmic specks.
scenes.visual: The entire Solar System graphic (as a small circle) is shown being dwarfed multiple times by the vast M87* event horizon, illustrating it fitting inside more than 4 times.

script.thumbnail_text: M87* vs. Our Solar System: Cosmic Scale!
